summary:
The Secure America Act provides supplemental appropriations for fiscal year 2026 to the Department of Homeland Security, primarily for border security and immigration enforcement. It divides its funding into two titles, administered through the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs and the Committee on the Judiciary.

Under Title I, U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) receives $9.55 billion to hire, pay, train, and equip Border Patrol agents and support personnel for functions other than immigration and customs enforcement. None of these funds may be used for processing coordinators after October 31, 2028. U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) receives $7.45 billion for Homeland Security Investigations agents and support personnel, also for non-immigration and non-customs enforcement functions. This includes $108.5 million specifically for additional child exploitation investigators and forensic analysts to identify and rescue victims of child sexual exploitation. CBP also receives $3.45 billion for border security technology, including nonintrusive inspection equipment, air and marine assets, surveillance technologies, biometric entry-exit systems, counter-fentanyl efforts, and other mission support not related to immigration or customs enforcement. The bill restricts funding for surveillance towers along the southwest and northern borders to only those systems that CBP has accepted as having autonomous capabilities. A final Title I appropriation provides $2.5 billion to the Secretary of Homeland Security for these same purposes.

Under Title II, CBP receives $13.02 billion specifically to carry out immigration enforcement activities, including hiring and equipping agents and support staff. ICE receives $31.075 billion for immigration enforcement, covering personnel, transportation for removals, information technology, facility and fleet maintenance, agreements with state and local authorities under section 287(g) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, and the Office of the Principal Legal Advisor. At least $350 million is directed toward arresting certain criminal aliens in jurisdictions that are not qualified cooperating jurisdictions—defined as those without a section 287(g) agreement or a filed certification of compliance with specific information-sharing laws—and prohibits using those funds to release such individuals into the community except as required by existing law. Tribal governments are excluded from being treated as non-cooperating jurisdictions. An additional $2.5 billion is appropriated to the Secretary for these Title II activities. All funds remain available through September 30, 2029, and the Act was approved on June 10, 2026.
